1: Ps 115: Anti-Idol Polemic and the God Who Acts — 'Not to Us, O LORD.' Ps 115 opens with the most radical self-effacing statement in the Psalter: 'Not to us, O LORD, not to us, but to your name give glory.' The glory of YHWH is the psalm's organizing concern, and the contrast between YHWH (who acts, vv3,12-16) and idols (who cannot act, vv4-8) is the rhetorical engine. 'Not to us, O LORD, not to us, but to your name give glory, for the sake of your steadfast love and your faithfulness!' (lo lanu YHWH lo lanu ki leshimcha ten kavod al chasdecha al amitecha) — the double 'not to us' is emphatic: the people disclaim any credit for YHWH's acts. The ground for divine action is YHWH's own chesed and emet — not Israel's merit.
2: 'Why should the nations say, Where is their God?' — the nations' taunt: ayeh na Eloheihem (where now is their God?). This is the theodicy question posed by Israel's enemies (also Psalms 42:3; Psalms 79:10): if YHWH is Israel's God, why is Israel in distress? The question assumes that a god's worth is measured by his people's visible prosperity and power. The psalm responds by inverting the terms: the question reveals not YHWH's absence but the questioners' false theological assumptions.
3: 'Our God is in the heavens; he does all that he pleases' — 'in the heavens' (bashamayim) — the answer to 'where is their God?': in the heavens, above all earthly measurement. 'Does all that he pleases' (kol asher chaphetz asah) — unrestricted divine sovereignty. The contrast with the idols (vv4-8) is immediate: YHWH does whatever he wants; the idols can do nothing at all. The nations' taunt is answered by the assertion of radical divine freedom.
4: 'Their idols are silver and gold, the work of human hands' — 'the work of human hands' (ma'aseh yedei adam) — the idol-polemic's core argument: idols are human artifacts, not divine beings. The same argument appears in Isaiah 40:18-20; Psalms 44:9-20; Jeremiah 10:1-16. The silver and gold of the idols are the most valuable human materials — yet they are inert matter shaped by human craftsmen. The very costliness of the materials exposes the absurdity: the most precious human resources employed to create a powerless object.
5: 'They have mouths, but do not speak; eyes, but do not see' — the sensory catalogue of idol-incapacity: mouths without speech, eyes without sight. The idol has the form of the human senses — the senses that covenant relationship with YHWH requires (hearing his word, seeing his works) — but none of the function. The idol's face is a parody of the 'face of YHWH' (panim) that worshipers seek.
6: 'They have ears, but do not hear; noses, but do not smell' — ears without hearing, noses without smelling: the catalogue continues. 'Hearing' (shama) in covenant theology is the fundamental act of covenant responsiveness — the Shema ('hear, O Israel') frames everything. An idol that cannot hear cannot receive a prayer. A nose that cannot smell means the sacrificial offerings are not received: the covenant communication is one-directional and therefore no communication at all.
7: 'They have hands, but do not feel; feet, but do not walk; and they do not make a sound in their throat' — hands without touch, feet without movement, throats without sound: the final sensory failures. The idol cannot act (hands), cannot come (feet), cannot respond (throat). The climactic list reveals the idol as the anti-YHWH: where YHWH hears, speaks, walks with his people (covenant walk-language: Leviticus 26:12), the idol does none of these.
8: 'Those who make them become like them; so do all who trust in them' — the most devastating point of the idol polemic: worshipers become like their gods. 'Those who make them become like them' (kemothem yihyu oseihem) — the ontological contagion of idolatry. The idol-maker shapes the idol in his own image, but in worshiping it, he shapes himself in the idol's image: deaf, blind, mute, unable to walk. This is the anthropological corollary of the theological point: worship forms the worshiper. To trust in the incapacitated idols is to share their incapacity.
9: 'O Israel, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield' — the first of three covenant-community addresses (Israel, v9; house of Aaron, v10; those who fear YHWH, v11). 'Trust in the LORD' (batach bYHWH) — the alternative to idol-trust. 'He is their help and their shield' (ezram umaginam hu) — the two covenant images of divine protection: 'help' (ezer, the same word as Genesis 2:18, the 'helper' Adam needed; also the divine title in Psalms 121:2) and 'shield' (magen, the divine protection of Genesis 15:1).
10: 'O house of Aaron, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield' — the priestly guild addressed separately. The 'house of Aaron' (beit Aaron) — the Aaronic priests who serve in the temple. Their trust in YHWH rather than in the temple's ritual apparatus or the political power of their position is the calling. The refrain 'He is their help and their shield' is identical in all three addresses (vv9-11), creating a liturgical unity across the threefold community structure.
11: 'You who fear the LORD, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield' — the third address: 'those who fear the LORD' (yir'ei YHWH). This group may include the Gentile God-fearers who attached themselves to Israel's worship — the proselytes and sympathizers present at the pilgrimage festivals. Their inclusion alongside Israel and the priests creates a universal community of YHWH-fearers within the Hallel's context. The threefold invitation to trust mirrors the threefold blessing of vv12-13.
12: 'The LORD has remembered us; he will bless us; he will bless the house of Israel; he will bless the house of Aaron' — 'the LORD has remembered us' (YHWH zecharanu yevarech) — the covenant memory that activates covenant blessing. The threefold blessing corresponds to the threefold address (Israel, Aaron, those who fear YHWH). 'Has remembered us' is the past tense of covenant assurance: YHWH's memory of his covenant people is the ground for the future blessing.
13: '...he will bless those who fear the LORD, both the small and the great' — 'both the small and the great' (haqqetannim im hagedolim) — the blessing is not tiered by social status. The smallest (qatan) and the greatest (gadol) receive the same blessing from the same God. The social leveling of the covenant community: no covenant distinction between ranks of worshiper.
14: 'May the LORD give you increase, you and your children!' — the blessing of multiplication: yoseph YHWH aleichem (may the LORD increase you). The covenant multiplication blessing (Deuteronomy 28:4: 'blessed shall be the fruit of your womb') expressed as a Hallel prayer. The inclusion of 'your children' (veal beneichem) extends the blessing intergenerationally.
15: 'May you be blessed by the LORD, who made heaven and earth!' — 'who made heaven and earth' (oseh shamayim vaaretz) — the creator-title as the ground of the blessing. The one who made heaven and earth (all created space) is the one blessing the covenant community. The creator's blessing is guaranteed by his own creative power: he has resources to bless from his own creation.
16: 'The heavens are the LORD's heavens, but the earth he has given to the children of man' — 'the heavens are the LORD's' (hashamayim shamayim laYHWH) — YHWH's domain. 'The earth he has given to the children of man' (vehaaretz natan livnei adam) — the earth as humanity's domain by divine grant. This is the creation-mandate theology of Genesis 1:28: YHWH creates, then delegates earth-governance to humans (adam). The distinction between divine and human spheres does not mean divine absence from earth but explains why earth-governance is humanity's responsibility.
17: 'The dead do not praise the LORD, nor do any who go down into silence' — 'the dead do not praise the LORD' (lo hameitim yehalelu Yah) — one of the OT's clearest statements about death and praise. Those who have descended to Sheol (the realm of the dead, 'silence' dumah) cannot participate in the praise of the living community. This is the OT anthropology of the underworld: Sheol is a place of shadows and silence, not of covenant relationship (Psalms 6:5; Psalms 30:9; Psalms 88:10-12; Isaiah 38:18). The statement creates urgency for the living community: praise now, before death silences the voice.
18: 'But we will bless the LORD from this time forth and forevermore. Praise the LORD!' — the psalm's conclusion turns the urgency of v17 into a vow: 'we will bless the LORD' (vaanachnu nevarech Yah) — the living community taking responsibility for the praise that the dead cannot offer. 'From this time forth and forevermore' (me'atah vead olam) — the same temporal formula as Psalms 113:2, creating a bracket within the Egyptian Hallel. The final 'Hallelujah!' closes the anti-idol psalm with the affirmation that YHWH alone is worthy of the praise that idols can neither give nor receive.
